answer:
For Allama Iqbal in *Bang-e-Dara*, *qaum* (قوم)—translated here as "nation" or "people"—denotes a dynamic, organic collective bound by shared spiritual destiny, faith (especially Islam), history, and collective action, rather than mere geography, race, or static identity.[^1][^2] It is frequently depicted as a living body (*jism*), with individuals as its limbs (*aaza* or *uzv*), interdependent and animated by a collective soul or vision; the poet serves as its "seeing eye," voicing its pains and aspirations.[^3] This *qaum* is not passive: it must awaken, unify beyond sects, reject world-renunciation or division, and strive toward self-realization (*khudi*) and global caliphate through *ishq* (passionate love for God) and adherence to the Qur'an and Prophet Muhammad.[^4][^5] Iqbal critiques its contemporary decay—divided, imitative, and spiritually adrift—while invoking its glorious past as conquerors of falsehood and bearers of *tawhid* (Oneness).[^6]

### Tracing *qaum* Across *Bang-e-Dara*
Iqbal's usage evolves from localized Indian patriotism (Part 1) toward a universal Islamic *millat*/*ummah* (Part 3), with Part 2 offering fewer direct references but lamenting civilizational decline.[^7]

#### Part 1: Foundations in Homeland and Organic Unity
Early poems ground *qaum* in Indian soil (*watan*) as a sacred, multi-faith cradle of truth (*haq*), drawing Chishti, Nanak, Tatars, Turks, and Hijazis—a proto-national community predating modern borders.[^8] Here, *qaum* is vital for children to embrace as "homeland," evoking historical glory (e.g., Persian stars reborn in India, Sinai-like peaks).[^9]

It extends to communal guidance: a tombstone inscription warns against teaching *qaum* (*your own people*) world-denial (*tark-e-duniya*) or sectarianism (*firqa-bandi*), urging unity, courage, and poetry to ignite action.[^10]

Climactically, in "Shair" (*The Poet*), *qaum* is explicitly a "body, a living frame" (*jism... aaza-e-qaum*), with hands/feet as craft/trade, government as "graceful face," and the poet as its weeping eye when "one part aches."[^11]

#### Part 2: Implicit Decline
Mentions are sparse (e.g., active *qaumein*/*nations* contrast poetry's frivolity), but "Saqaliya" mourns "Hejazi civilization" as a grave, implying *qaum*'s burial under foreign dominance, urging tears to revive it in Hindustan.[^12]

#### Part 3: Crisis and Revival of the Muslim *Qalum*
*Qalum* becomes the beleaguered Muslim *millat*/*qaumiyat-e-Islam*, unified in "profit and loss," Prophet, faith, Haram, and Qur'an—yet fractured by sects, Western imitation, and ease.[^13][^14] In "Dil se jo baat..." (*A word from the heart...*), Iqbal laments: "*Qaum* mazhab se hai" (*A nation has a faith; if not, then what are you?*); without binding *jazb* (gravity), it scatters like stars.[^15] Ancestors embodied fearless truth; descendants lack Ali's strength or Uthman's wealth, selling graves while nations recall their *sadaaqat* (truthfulness).[^16] Revival demands passion over reason, Muhammad's fire to birth a "global caliphate."[^17]

"Shikwa" (*Complaint*) traces *qaum*'s fall: once sword-wielders of *tawhid* from China to Morocco, now mocked as "gone," their *shaan-e-jalali* (majesty of glory) eclipsed, yet capable of rebirth via Hijazi wine.[^18][^19]

Iqbal's *qaum* thus fuses Nietzschean vitalism, Islamic *ummah*, and anti-colonial fervor: a self-aware, faith-fueled organism destined to lead if revived.[^20]

[^1]: Multiple chapters, e.g., *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, ch. 39, [translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/39-shair/translation/).

[^2]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4, [¶ p-fd7840](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-fd7840).

[^3]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, ch. 39, [¶ p-6afdc8](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/39-shair/translation/#p-6afdc8).

[^4]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, ch. 38, [¶ p-7a6aba](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/38-sayed-ki-loh-e-turbat/translation/#p-7a6aba); *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4, [translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/).

[^5]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4, [¶ p-819a53](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-819a53).

[^6]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, ch. 20, [¶ p-5df518](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/20-shikwa/translation/#p-5df518) (transliteration snippet confirms *qaum*).

[^7]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 2*, ch. 2 (transliteration: *qaumein*).

[^8]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, ch. 26, [translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/26-hindustani-bachon-ka-qaumi-geet/translation/).

[^9]: Ibid., [¶¶ p-da6786, p-a9f72d](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/26-hindustani-bachon-ka-qaumi-geet/translation/#p-da6786).

[^10]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, ch. 38, [¶¶ p-7a6aba, p-72e543](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/38-sayed-ki-loh-e-turbat/translation/#p-7a6aba).

[^11]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, ch. 39, [¶¶ p-4562af–p-6afdc8](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/39-shair/translation/#p-4562af).

[^12]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 2*, ch. 21, [¶ p-08760e](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-08760e) (Hejazi *qaum* as civilization).

[^13]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4, [¶ p-fd7840](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-fd7840).

[^14]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, ch. 2 (transliteration: *qaumiyat-e-Islam*).

[^15]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4, [¶ p-7a9795](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-7a9795).

[^16]: Ibid., [¶¶ p-bf18c2, p-45a9df](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-bf18c2).

[^17]: Ibid., [¶ p-819a53](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-819a53).

[^18]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, ch. 20, [translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/20-shikwa/translation/).

[^19]: Ibid., [¶ p-847833](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/20-shikwa/translation/#p-847833).

[^20]: Corpus covers *Bang-e-Dara* comprehensively (100 chapters); no fuller trace elsewhere.
